literal: sense-faculty, sense-organ, power; literally 'belonging to Indra' or 'the power of Indra', hence a power or capacity of the embodied self
invariant_definition: *Indriya* is a sense-faculty: a capacity of the embodied self by which it perceives or acts upon the world. In the standard Sāṅkhya scheme inherited across the schools the *indriyas* number eleven: five faculties of knowledge (*jñānendriya* — hearing, touch, sight, taste, smell), five faculties of action (*karmendriya* — speech, grasping, locomotion, excretion, generation), and the *manas* (mind) as the eleventh, the internal faculty that coordinates the other ten. The *indriya* is not the gross physical organ (the eye-ball, the ear) but the functional power operating through it. A persistent hierarchy orders the levels of the person: the sense-objects (*artha*) are higher than the senses, *manas* higher than the senses, *buddhi* higher than *manas*, and the *ātman* higher than *buddhi* (Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.3.10) — the senses are thus instruments subordinate to the self, not the self. This hierarchy gives the *Bhagavad-Gītā*'s *sthitaprajña* teaching its structure: mastery of the senses is the practical mark of settled wisdom. The text's tortoise simile (2.58) — the sage who withdraws his senses from their objects as a tortoise draws in its limbs — and its repeated demand that the senses be brought under control with the mind fixed on the higher self (2.61, 2.64, 2.68) make *indriya*-restraint the visible discipline of the inwardly steady.

school_framing.shared_core: *Indriya* names **a functional sense-faculty of the embodied self — an instrument of knowing or acting, distinct from its gross organ, its object, the coordinating *manas*, and the self that owns it** (the eleven-fold scheme of Sāṅkhya-Kārikā 26-27; the hierarchy of Kaṭha 1.3.10). All Brāhmaṇical schools accept the structural role and the subordination of the senses to mind, intellect, and self.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Same M+Φ register, same scope-inside. Schools agree on the inventory and hierarchy of the *indriyas*; differences are minor and concern the metaphysical status of the faculties (evolutes of *prakṛti* in Sāṅkhya-Yoga, instruments of the dependent *jīva* in Vedānta) rather than the concept itself.

per_school.definition: The *indriyas* are evolutes of *prakṛti* arising from the sattvic aspect of *ahaṃkāra*: the five *buddhīndriyas* (faculties of knowledge) and five *karmendriyas* (faculties of action), with *manas* as the eleventh, sharing the nature of both since it is both a sense and an organ of action (SK 26-27). They function for the sake of the *puruṣa*, presenting the field of objects to the *buddhi*; the senses act in concert, like a lamp, to serve the purpose of the witnessing self (SK 31, 36-37). They are firmly instruments, never the conscious self.

Pātañjala Yoga inherits the Sāṅkhya inventory and treats the senses soteriologically: *pratyāhāra*, the fifth limb, is the withdrawal of the *indriyas* from their objects, their conforming to the nature of the *citta* when disjoined from contact (YS 2.54). Mastery of the senses follows (YS 2.55), and the higher *saṃyama* on the very process of sensing yields control over the senses (YS 3.47-48). Sense-restraint is thus an explicit stage of the disciplined path, prerequisite to inward concentration — the same logic the *Gītā* compresses into the tortoise image.

Vedānta receives the eleven-fold scheme and the Kaṭha hierarchy and makes sense-control the operational test of the *sthitaprajña*. The senses are restless and forcibly carry away even the striving mind (2.60); the disciplined sage draws them in from every side as a tortoise withdraws its limbs (2.58), and holds them in check with his thought fixed on the higher self or the Lord (2.61, 2.64, 2.68). The self is the owner and witness of the senses, not their captive; liberation requires that the *indriyas* be governed by the steadied *buddhi* rather than the *buddhi* be dragged by the *indriyas* (2.67).

translator_note: 'Sense' or 'sense-organ' is acceptable, but note two pitfalls. First, *indriya* is the functional faculty, not the physical organ, so 'organ' can mislead toward anatomy. Second, the standard scheme includes faculties of action (*karmendriya*) and the mind (*manas*) as the eleventh, so 'the five senses' undercounts. When the *Gītā* speaks of withdrawing or controlling the *indriyas*, it means the whole instrument-set subordinate to mind and self, not merely perception.
